topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone screen, a familiar little chime that usually signaled a new assignment or a group chat update. But this time, the profile picture was Mr. Harrison, my notoriously stern history teacher. My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow intake of air that felt like swallowing glass. I clicked it open, expecting a professional photo, maybe a picture of a lecture hall. Instead, there was a selfie. A slightly blurry picture of him smiling, wearing a ridiculous novelty t-shirt, taken clearly from the back of my desk during a particularly dull class. My hands started to shake, the phone slipping slightly in my grip. Every nerve ending in my body seemed to tighten, pulling me inward. I quickly minimized the app, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. I stared at the screen for a full minute, the silence of my bedroom amplifying the frantic drumming inside my chest. What had I just seen?

[story 2]

It happened while I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly looking for something distracting. A profile picture caught my eye—Mr. Davies, my English literature instructor. My stomach plummeted. It was a photo taken at a local coffee shop, laughing with a group of students, but the angle was undeniably from behind me. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. He was following me. A cold sweat broke out on my forehead, instantly dampening the back of my neck. I dropped my phone onto my bedspread, the sound muffled by the fabric. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force my breathing to slow down, but it wouldn't. I could feel my palms growing slick, an uncontrollable tremor running up my arms. I needed to get out, to disappear, but my feet felt rooted to the floor.

[story 3]

I was reviewing my notes when the alert flashed. It was from Professor Albright, the professor who graded everything with an unnerving intensity. I hesitated, my finger hovering over the notification. When I clicked, the image displayed was a recent snapshot from a campus event. In the background, slightly out of focus, was me, sitting alone at a library table. And there, standing just beyond the frame, was Professor Albright, looking directly at the camera, a knowing smirk playing on his lips. A wave of nausea washed over me. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, making the room swim. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I snatched the phone, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, and threw it onto the desk as if it were suddenly burning something.
